metadata.description: Paired neuroethics and neurosecurity guardrails for QIF research. Each neuroethics constraint from published literature maps to a neurosecurity scope limit that QIF enforces.

source.author: Morse
source.journal: Ohio State J. Criminal Law / Mercer Law Review (2011)

neuroethics.constraint: Neural correlates do not prove causation or eliminate agency. Neuroscience findings are routinely overclaimed when applied to law and policy.
neuroethics.violation: Brain activity X proves cognitive state Y
neuroethics.correct: Brain activity X is associated with / correlates with cognitive state Y

neurosecurity.scope: We score signal-level interference, not mental states. NISS measures physical amplitude disruption, not 'thought harm.'

source.author: Poldrack
source.work: Can cognitive processes be inferred from neuroimaging data?

neuroethics.constraint: Brain region activation does not uniquely identify a cognitive process. Signal detection does not entail mental-state identification.
neuroethics.violation: Activation of X means the person is experiencing Y
neuroethics.correct: Activation of X is associated with multiple cognitive processes including Y

neurosecurity.scope: Signal detection does not entail mental-state identification. TARA catalogs physical interference patterns, not cognitive content.

neuroethics.constraint: Brain images create three biases: neuro-realism (overcredibility), neuro-essentialism (reducing people to brains), neuropolicy (premature policy justification). Check all three.
neuroethics.violation: BCI threats reveal that brain data reveals identity
neuroethics.correct: Neural signals are partial, noisy representations, not transparent read-outs of selfhood

neurosecurity.scope: Documentation must not frame BCI threats as 'brain data reveals identity.' Neural signals are partial, noisy representations, not transparent read-outs of selfhood.

neuroethics.constraint: Existing human rights may cover neural data without inventing new neurorights. Don't assume new rights are needed without showing the gap.
neuroethics.violation: We need 15 new neurorights for BCI security
neuroethics.correct: We extend Mental Privacy and Mental Integrity with technical depth, not new rights. Five rights, not fifteen.

neurosecurity.scope: We extend Mental Privacy and Mental Integrity with technical depth, not new rights. Five rights, not fifteen.

neuroethics.constraint: 'Mental privacy' and 'mental integrity' lack agreed operational definitions. Never use them as settled concepts.
neuroethics.violation: NISS protects mental privacy (as if the term is well-defined)
neuroethics.correct: NISS measures operationally defined properties: signal amplitude, frequency, coherence

neurosecurity.scope: We define operationally measurable properties (signal amplitude, frequency, coherence) rather than philosophically contested mental states.

neuroethics.constraint: Current BCIs cannot 'read thoughts.' Decoded outputs are selected from known lists, require user cooperation, and need algorithm training.
neuroethics.violation: Attackers can read victims' thoughts via BCI
neuroethics.correct: Threat models must distinguish between current capabilities and projected future capabilities. We catalog what is technically possible, not what is science fiction.

neurosecurity.scope: Threat models must distinguish between current capabilities and projected future capabilities. We catalog what is technically possible, not what is science fiction.

neuroethics.constraint: Security framing of neurotech risks enabling surveillance. Always pair threat descriptions with governance constraints.
neuroethics.violation: Here's how to attack a BCI (without defensive framing)
neuroethics.correct: The framework specifies defensive clinical protections. Offensive applications are explicitly out of scope.

neurosecurity.scope: The framework specifies defensive clinical protections. Offensive applications are explicitly out of scope. The threat catalog exists to inform defense, not enable attack.

source.author: Vul et al. / Eklund et al.

neuroethics.constraint: Neuroimaging studies have high false-positive rates. Never cite fMRI correlations as strong evidence without noting methodological limitations.
neuroethics.violation: Neuroimaging study X proves that BCI attack Y causes Z
neuroethics.correct: Claims citing neuroimaging findings as ground truth must account for demonstrated validity failures in the underlying methods.

neurosecurity.scope: Claims citing neuroimaging findings as ground truth must account for demonstrated validity failures in the underlying methods.

componentBounds.does: Measures physical signal disruption: amplitude deviation, frequency shift, coherence degradation
doesNot: Predict psychiatric diagnoses (corresponds to diagnostic categories for threat modeling)

componentBounds.does: Catalogs physical interference patterns, maps attack techniques to signal-level effects
doesNot: Catalog cognitive content or mental states
Claim attacks that exceed current BCI capabilities without flagging as projected

componentBounds.does: Specifies post-quantum cryptographic protections for neural data in transit and at rest
doesNot: Claim adoption by any standards body
Guarantee protection beyond the protocol's technical scope

componentBounds.does: Detects anomalous signal patterns via phase variance, temporal entropy, gain fluctuation, spectral drift
doesNot: Uniquely identify cognitive processes from signal activation
Serve as a clinical diagnostic instrument
componentBounds.status: proposed, validated on EEG data, not independently replicated

overclaim.label: Overclaim & Scope
overclaim.description: Constraints on what QIF can claim about its scope and capabilities

methodology.label: Methodology
methodology.description: Constraints on how QIF interprets and cites scientific evidence

framing.label: Framing
framing.description: Constraints on how QIF presents threats and capabilities
